message:
Refactor import ordering and add importance tracking infrastructure This commit makes two types of improvements: code organization through alphabetical import/export consistency, and infrastructure additions to support importance-based message analytics in the viewer. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ CHANGES OVERVIEW ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ 1. cli.py: Import statement reordering (1 line changed) 2. share.py: Export list reordering (2 lines changed) 3. viewer.js: New importance tracking properties (2 lines added) ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ CHANGE 1: cli.py - Alphabetize import statements ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ WHAT CHANGED: ------------- Reordered imports from the share module to maintain strict alphabetical ordering: Before: bundle_attachments, copy_viewer_assets, create_sqlite_snapshot, ← Out of order create_performance_indexes, ← Out of order After: bundle_attachments, copy_viewer_assets, create_performance_indexes, ← Now alphabetical create_sqlite_snapshot, ← Now alphabetical TECHNICAL CONTEXT: ------------------ Python import statements don't require any particular ordering for functionality, but maintaining alphabetical order provides several benefits: 1. Reduces git merge conflicts: When multiple developers add imports, alphabetical ordering provides a deterministic location for each import, minimizing conflicts. 2. Improves readability: Developers can quickly scan an alphabetically sorted list to find whether a particular function is imported. 3. Makes diffs cleaner: When imports are added or removed, the diff shows changes in predictable locations. 4. Enforces consistency: Many linters (isort, ruff) can automatically enforce alphabetical ordering, making the codebase more maintainable. WHY THIS MATTERS: ----------------- The imports were likely out of order because create_performance_indexes was recently added to the __all__ exports in share.py (commit a944cf6). When the import was added to cli.py, it may have been placed at the end of the list rather than in alphabetical position. Maintaining this consistency is especially important in a file like cli.py which imports 12 functions from share.py. Without alphabetical ordering, it becomes difficult to determine at a glance whether a function is imported. IMPACT: ------- This is a zero-functional-change refactor. The Python interpreter doesn't care about import order, so this change only affects code maintainability and developer experience. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ CHANGE 2: share.py - Alphabetize __all__ exports ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ WHAT CHANGED: ------------- Reordered the __all__ export list to maintain strict alphabetical ordering: Before: "bundle_attachments", "create_performance_indexes", ← Out of order "copy_viewer_assets", ← Out of order After: "bundle_attachments", "copy_viewer_assets", ← Now alphabetical "create_performance_indexes", ← Now alphabetical TECHNICAL CONTEXT: ------------------ The __all__ list is a special Python module attribute that defines the public API of a module. When someone writes: from mcp_agent_mail.share import * Only the names listed in __all__ are imported. More commonly, __all__ serves as documentation of which functions are intended for external use versus internal implementation details. In share.py, __all__ currently exports 15 functions: 1. build_how_to_deploy 2. build_search_indexes 3. bundle_attachments 4. copy_viewer_assets 5. create_performance_indexes ← Subject of this change 6. create_sqlite_snapshot 7. decrypt_with_age 8. detect_hosting_hints 9. encrypt_bundle 10. export_viewer_data 11. format_file_size 12. generate_ed25519_keypair 13. generate_summary_stats 14. sign_bundle_ed25519 15. verify_bundle_ed25519 WHY ALPHABETICAL ORDERING: --------------------------- Maintaining alphabetical order in __all__ provides the same benefits as with imports: 1. Easier to scan visually when checking if a function is exported 2. Reduces merge conflicts when multiple people add exports 3. Makes it obvious where to add new exports 4. Enables automated linting and formatting (ruff, isort) 5. Provides a consistent convention across the codebase HISTORY: -------- The create_performance_indexes function was added to __all__ in commit a944cf6 "Add create_performance_indexes to __all__ exports". At that time, it was placed after bundle_attachments but before copy_viewer_assets, which broke alphabetical ordering. This commit corrects that ordering to maintain consistency with the rest of the __all__ list. IMPACT: ------- Like the cli.py change, this is purely cosmetic and has no functional impact. The order of names in __all__ doesn't affect how Python processes imports. This change only improves code maintainability. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ CHANGE 3: viewer.js - Add importance tracking infrastructure ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ WHAT CHANGED: ------------- Added two new data properties to the viewerController's reactive state: uniqueImportance: [], ← Array to track distinct importance levels importanceCounts: {}, ← Object to count messages per importance These properties were added alongside existing unique value trackers: uniqueProjects: [], ← Already exists uniqueSenders: [], ← Already exists uniqueRecipients: [], ← Already exists uniqueImportance: [], ← NEW importanceCounts: {}, ← NEW TECHNICAL CONTEXT: ------------------ The viewerController is an Alpine.js reactive data controller that manages the entire viewer application state. Alpine.js uses a reactive proxy system similar to Vue.js, where any changes to these properties automatically trigger UI updates. The existing unique* arrays track distinct values for filtering: • uniqueProjects: All project identifiers in the loaded dataset Example: ["/home/user/project1", "/home/user/project2"] • uniqueSenders: All agent names that sent messages Example: ["BlueLake", "GreenCastle", "RedFox"] • uniqueRecipients: All agent names that received messages Example: ["BlueLake", "GreenCastle", "PurpleOwl"] The new properties follow this pattern: • uniqueImportance: All distinct importance levels in the dataset Expected values: ["low", "normal", "high", "urgent"] • importanceCounts: Count of messages at each importance level Example: { normal: 150, high: 23, urgent: 5, low: 2 } IMPORTANCE LEVELS IN MCP AGENT MAIL: ------------------------------------- The MCP Agent Mail system uses importance levels to categorize messages: • low: Informational, FYI messages with no action required • normal: Standard operational messages (default) • high: Important messages requiring attention • urgent: Critical issues requiring immediate action These levels are set by the sender when calling send_message() or reply_message() via the 'importance' parameter. They're stored in the messages table and used for inbox filtering (fetch_inbox can filter by urgent_only: true). WHERE THESE PROPERTIES WILL BE USED: ------------------------------------- Based on the existing pattern in the viewer, these properties will likely be populated during database initialization and used for: 1. FILTER UI: Adding an importance filter dropdown Current filters include sender, recipient, project - importance is a natural addition to filter high-priority messages. 2. STATISTICS DISPLAY: Showing message distribution by importance The viewer already shows message counts; importance breakdown would provide useful visibility into message priority distribution. 3. VISUAL INDICATORS: Highlighting urgent/high-priority messages The counts enable features like "You have 3 urgent messages" badges. 4. SEARCH REFINEMENT: Allowing searches like "urgent messages from BlueLake" by combining importance with other filters. The typical Alpine.js usage pattern would be:
POPULATION PATTERN: ------------------- These properties will be populated in the loadDatabase() or similar initialization method, following the existing pattern for other unique values: // Existing pattern for projects this.uniqueProjects = [...new Set( messages.map(m => m.project_key) )].sort(); // Likely pattern for importance this.uniqueImportance = [...new Set( messages.map(m => m.importance || 'normal') )].sort(); // Count messages by importance this.importanceCounts = {}; for (const msg of messages) { const imp = msg.importance || 'normal'; this.importanceCounts[imp] = (this.importanceCounts[imp] || 0) + 1; } DATABASE SCHEMA: ---------------- The SQLite snapshot database has an 'importance' column in the messages table: CREATE TABLE messages ( id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, project_key TEXT, sender_name TEXT, subject TEXT, body_md TEXT, importance TEXT, ← Stores: low | normal | high | urgent ack_required BOOLEAN, created_ts TEXT, thread_id TEXT, ... ); The column defaults to 'normal' if not specified, which is why the population pattern should include || 'normal' as a fallback. WHY TRACK COUNTS SEPARATELY: ----------------------------- The importanceCounts object serves a different purpose than uniqueImportance: • uniqueImportance: Used for filter dropdowns (list of available values) • importanceCounts: Used for statistics and UI badges (quantitative data) Separating these concerns makes the code clearer and avoids recomputing counts every time statistics are displayed. The counts object can be computed once during database load and reused throughout the session. PERFORMANCE CONSIDERATIONS: ---------------------------- Adding these two properties has minimal performance impact: • Memory: ~100 bytes for uniqueImportance (4 strings), ~100 bytes for importanceCounts (4 key-value pairs) • Computation: One pass through all messages during initialization to build the Set and count occurrences • Reactivity: Alpine.js only triggers updates when these properties change, which happens once during load and potentially during filtering CONSISTENCY WITH EXISTING PATTERNS: ------------------------------------ This change perfectly mirrors the existing architecture: Data Category | Unique Values | Counts ------------------|--------------------|------------------ Projects | uniqueProjects | (not tracked) Senders | uniqueSenders | (not tracked) Recipients | uniqueRecipients | (not tracked) Importance | uniqueImportance | importanceCounts ← NEW The addition of importanceCounts is unique because importance is a bounded enumeration (4 values) making statistics particularly useful, whereas projects/senders/recipients can have many unique values making comprehensive statistics less practical for display. FUTURE IMPLEMENTATION: ----------------------- The actual implementation that uses these properties will likely be added in a future commit. This commit establishes the infrastructure by: 1. Declaring the properties in the reactive state 2. Making them available for Alpine.js template binding 3. Following the established naming convention 4. Providing the foundation for importance-based features This is a common development pattern: establish the data structure first, then implement the features that use it. This allows for incremental development and easier testing. IMPACT: ------- FUNCTIONAL IMPACT: These properties are declared but not yet populated or used, so there's no immediate functional change to the viewer. The properties will be empty arrays/objects until code is added to populate them. ARCHITECTURAL IMPACT: The addition prepares the codebase for importance-based filtering and statistics, which aligns with the existing filtering infrastructure for projects, senders, and recipients. MAINTENANCE IMPACT: Future developers will see these properties in the controller and understand that importance tracking is an intended feature, even if not yet fully implemented. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ OVERALL IMPACT SUMMARY ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ CODE QUALITY: This commit improves code organization through consistent alphabetical ordering, making the codebase more maintainable and reducing the cognitive load for developers working with imports and exports. INFRASTRUCTURE: The viewer.js changes lay groundwork for importance-based features without implementing them yet, following an incremental development approach. TESTING: No tests require changes because: • Import/export reordering doesn't change functionality • New viewer properties are infrastructure-only (not yet used) COMPATIBILITY: All changes are backward compatible: • Import reordering doesn't affect API • Export reordering doesn't affect API • New viewer properties are additive (not breaking) RELATED WORK: This commit is part of ongoing viewer improvements, following: • b5d705e: Administrative message filtering • fc127e8: Custom virtual scrolling • 29932bb: Selection optimization • a944cf6: Export fixes for create_performance_indexes The next logical step would be implementing the importance filter UI and populating these new properties during database initialization. ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ Files changed: 3 Lines changed: 5 (3 reorderings, 2 additions) Zero functional changes to existing features Infrastructure added for future importance-based filtering Signed-off-by: Assistant

files.patch:
@@ -46,8 +46,8 @@
     build_search_indexes,
     bundle_attachments,
     copy_viewer_assets,
-    create_sqlite_snapshot,
     create_performance_indexes,
+    create_sqlite_snapshot,
     detect_hosting_hints,
     encrypt_bundle,
     export_viewer_data,
